figures.caption: Figure 5: Hyperparameter sensitivity analysis on three datasets.
Table 1: Overall performance comparison on three datasets.

Table 3: Average dataset statistics per user. For seen users, Cu is sampled from training data per epoch, and the full training set serves as Cu at evaluation. For unseen users, only Cu is provided at inference to estimate user weights. Both settings are evaluated on held-out Du.
Figure 3: In-depth analysis of unseen users in PRISM. VRF maintains superior accuracy across all user groups (a–c) while significantly reducing adaptation latency (d).
Figure 2: Overall framework of VRF. Best viewed in color.
Table 2: Ablation study on VRF across three datasets.

Figure 4: Case studies and the learned preference space on PRISM (K=8). (a) Four users with varying data scarcity and preference diversity. n is the reference set size. Bars show the preference distribution classified by Claude Sonnet 4.6 (see Appendix D.1 for details). (b) Learned preference space. Stars and shaded regions denote the preference bases. For user ellipses, the center point represents wu, and the size reflects σu.
Figure 6: In-depth comparison on PersonalLLM (α=0.001) and TL;DR (n=150) unseen users.
